Mike Tyson’s Health Challenges and the Jake Paul Fight: Did He Really Belong in the Ring?

In November 2020, at age 54, Mike Tyson returned to the ring for an exhibition match against Roy Jones Jr., where he surprised fans with his exceptional physical condition and agility. Fast forward to 2024, Tyson faced Jake Paul at 58 years old, but this time, his performance was visibly diminished. The reason? A series of serious health challenges leading up to the fight.

---

Health Challenges Post-2020

- Severe Ulcer and Near-Death Experience:
In June 2024, Tyson suffered from a life-threatening ulcer that led to significant blood loss. He needed eight blood transfusions and lost 25 pounds in a short period. Tyson himself admitted, “I lost half my blood, I almost died.” Such a dramatic health crisis just months before the fight undoubtedly impacted his conditioning.

- Sepsis:
Reports suggest Tyson battled sepsis—a dangerous condition resulting from severe infection—that can cause long-term damage to the body and greatly affect physical performance.

These challenges would take a toll on any athlete, let alone one in their late 50s attempting to compete at an elite level. To blame Tyson’s diminished performance solely on the passage of time since his last fight is to ignore these critical health factors.
---

Regulatory and Ethical Concerns

The fight took place in Texas, where the Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation (TDLR) oversees professional boxing. Fighters over 38 must pass rigorous medical tests, including EEG and EKG assessments, to ensure brain and heart health. While Tyson may have met the minimal requirements to compete, critics have questioned whether the full extent of his recent health issues was properly considered.

Sports commentators, including Chris Mannix of *Sports Illustrated*, called out the decision, questioning how Tyson was cleared to fight so soon after his ulcer and weight loss. “Does anyone care Tyson almost died?” asked Mannix.
---

Commercialization Over Integrity?

The bout was promoted as a high-profile event streamed on Netflix, with massive corporate backing. Key players included:

- Netflix: The exclusive streaming platform for the event.
- AT&T Stadium: The venue hosting the fight.
- Promotional Companies: Matchroom Sport, DAZN Boxing, and other entities responsible for organizing and marketing the event.

Critics argue that the fight wasn’t about the love of boxing or respecting its legends but a spectacle designed to maximize profits at the expense of an aging icon. Allowing a recently ill, 58-year-old Mike Tyson to step into the ring against a much younger, healthier Jake Paul reflects the growing trend of prioritizing money and clout over safety and sportsmanship.
---

A Disturbing Trend in Boxing

Imagine a world where health and skill no longer matter—where fighters are lured or pushed into the ring despite serious health concerns that should make the proposition unthinkable in the first place, but it is still done, nonetheless, simply because it benefits the sponsors and streaming platforms. That’s exactly what we saw with this fight.

It’s not just disrespectful to Tyson, a legend of the sport, but it undermines the integrity of boxing itself and may undermine the integrity of other sports where we have seen similar things occur. Boxing risks becoming a laughingstock where anyone can fight anyone, regardless of ability, experience, or even medical readiness, so long as it’s profitable. It becomes a grotesque spectacle at the expense of a beloved icon whose already diminished health and the sport’s integrity.

---

Conclusion: Was It Worth It?

Mike Tyson fought valiantly despite the odds stacked against him. But the real question isn’t whether Tyson lost—it’s whether he should have been in the ring at all. The companies behind this event profited off a man who had no business fighting, turning a cash grab into a grotesque spectacle.

What do you think? Besides Jake Paul, did Netflix, the organizers, and promoters of this event cross the line? Or is it fine to say it’s just business as usual?

Title: Mike Tyson vs. Jake Paul was RIGGED.
Description: twitch.tv/selloutsiah twitch.tv/siahtheclown_.
Comment: Nope.  There’s no upside for Mike to pretend to look that bad.  He WAS exactly what we saw.  And yea, everyone could see Jake Paul was coasting.  But what was the alternative?  Caving Tyson’s head in?  No upside for that either.  Tyson had a life threatening health situation in July that required surgery on his abdomen.  If you look at the video of Tyson vs Roy Jones Jr, even though it was only an exhibition, you can SEE the difference in his mobility.  His ability to weave and bob from his waist was WAYYY better than what we saw last night.  Even if he WAS throwing the fight, he couldn’t fake the physical limitations we saw in this fight.  Dude couldn’t move.. couldn’t keep his balance, couldn’t bend at the waist then explode upward and forward like he was doing just 4 years ago.  It’s not impossible that they scripted it.. and I wouldn’t put NOTHING past Paul.. but Tyson couldn’t give that guy a fight last night… he wasn’t faking that.

Comment:
The Spectacle of Mike Tyson vs. Jake Paul: A Mirror of Our Times

It’s November 16, 2024. Mike Tyson, an icon of boxing, steps into the ring once again, this time against Jake Paul—a social media influencer turned boxer. On the surface, it’s another big-ticket fight designed to entertain, to grab headlines, to rake in millions. But if you look deeper, what this fight truly reflects is the hollow, grotesque reality of the age we live in—a reality where nothing is sacred anymore, not even the legacy of a man like Tyson.
---

Mike Tyson is 58 years old. Only months ago, he nearly died. A life-threatening ulcer robbed him of half his blood, left him 25 pounds lighter, and barely hanging on. And yet, here he was, in the ring. Why? Not because he was ready. Not because he wanted to. But because this was a spectacle designed not for meaning, not for honor, not for respect—but for money, clout, and empty fame.

Some people see Tyson in that ring and applaud the fact that he showed up. They see the man, the fighter, the survivor. But far too many didn’t. They were stuck on the surface—caught up in their own shallow expectations. They wanted a fight, a bloodbath, a knockout. When they didn’t get it, they booed. They mocked. They called the match boring, even going as far as to ridicule Tyson himself.
---

An Icon Reduced to a Commodity

Mike Tyson is not a commodity. He is not a monkey with clashing cymbals, performing tricks to entertain a bloodthirsty crowd. He is a human being—a man with a story, with struggles, with a legacy. But in this grotesque new age, none of that matters. Tyson’s presence in that ring, after battling sepsis and an ulcer, was monumental. But the crowd, and even some commentators, couldn’t care less.

Instead, they fixated on what the fight wasn’t. It wasn’t brutal enough. It didn’t meet their shallow, violent expectations. A female commentator expressed concern during Amanda Serrano’s fight, noting she was bleeding, only for another commentator to gleefully confirm, “Oh yeah, she is,” as if that was the highlight. Another remarked how two-minute rounds in women’s boxing were great because it could lead to more knockouts—because, apparently, that’s all that matters now.

This isn’t about boxing anymore. It’s about turning people—human beings—into objects. Into disposable tools for profit, for entertainment, for a momentary fix of excitement - even if this means more suffering for those who cop the expense for our satisfaction (if we are even satisfied, that is..) It’s about reducing the sacred into the superficial, the meaningful into the meaningless. What an Age to live in: where we dance on the surface, barely alive.
---

The Age of Surface Over Substance

Jake Paul, the self-made content creator, is the perfect face for this era. He represents everything this new age stands for: appearances over integrity, style over content, surface over substance. Like so many others in this digital age, it’s not about what he’s doing, it’s about how it looks. It’s about crafting an image, a brand, a persona that can sell tickets, go viral, and rake in dollars.

But this fight wasn’t just about Jake Paul. It was about the people watching—the booing crowd, the jabbing commentators, the online critics. It was about us. Because this spectacle is a reflection of the age we’ve created. An age where nothing is sacred, and everything—people, legacy, meaning—can be bought and sold. It’s about a culture where there are all manner of egoic beasts parading as charitable role-models, feigning depth with twinkling smiles, while behind closed doors, it’s all about selfishness, ruthlessness, manipulation and exploitation.

Tyson’s legacy didn’t matter. His health didn’t matter. His presence, in and of itself, should have been enough to applaud, for each and every round.. but it wasn’t. People didn’t see him for what he was—a living legend overcoming the adversity ailment months prior and Showing UP nonetheless! They only saw what he wasn’t—a 20-year-old in peak condition, delivering a bloody spectacle for their amusement.
---

The True Loss

What we witnessed wasn’t just Tyson losing a fight. It was a loss for all of us. It was a loss of respect, of integrity, of meaning. Boxing, once a sacred sport of honor and skill - like other sports and even Industries - is becoming a laughingstock, void of soul and ethical backbone. Icons are reduced to commodities, lured or pushed into the ring not because they should be there, but because their presence is profitable. Tyson shouldn’t have been in that ring. And the fact that he was says more about us than it does about him.
---

So, what do you see when you look at this fight? Do you see a man, a legend, standing against all odds? Or do you see only what you didn’t get—blood, knockouts, chaos? Because what you see says more about you than it does about the fight with Mike Tyson. And that’s the uncomfortable truth of the world we’re living in. Because who you are? Who they are? Doesn't matter - as long as things Appear As Expected.
